course: CMU 21-254: Linear Algebra and Vector Calculus for Engineers
audience: SI leader teaching live on a whiteboard. This is not a student worksheet.
toneRule: Keep explanations very simple, verbal, and board-friendly. Avoid textbook phrasing unless the problem needs it.
title: Scalar Functions, Partials, Gradients, and Tangent Planes
sessionGoal: Students should be able to compute partial derivatives, use gradients for directional derivatives, and build tangent planes / linear approximations.
quickStartIfUnprepared: Start with the sentence: a scalar function takes in a point and outputs a number.
Do Problem 1 if students need gradient and directional derivative practice.
Do Problem 2 to connect tangent planes with linear approximation.
Do Problem 3 if optimization/classification is coming up soon.
sayThisFirst: A scalar function \(f(x,y)\) gives a number, like height or temperature.
A partial derivative means change one variable and freeze the other.
The gradient \(\nabla f\) points in the direction of fastest increase.
A directional derivative is the gradient dotted with a unit direction.
A tangent plane is the best linear approximation near a point.
boardPlan: Show directional derivative as \(\nabla f\cdot u\).
Do one tangent plane / approximation problem.
Finish with a critical point classification example if time allows.
keyFormulas: Directional derivative in unit direction \(u\): \(D_uf=\nabla f\cdot u\).
commonMistakes: Forgetting the direction vector must be a unit vector.
Plugging into the tangent plane before computing both partials.
Using the gradient as a point instead of a vector.
Forgetting the second derivative test can be inconclusive if \(D=0\).
notationWarnings: Use \(D_u f\) for directional derivative, where \(u\) should be a unit vector.
For tangent planes, clearly label the base point \((a,b)\).
For critical points, solve \(f_x=0\) and \(f_y=0\) together.

problems.title: Gradient and directional derivative
problems.studentPromptLatex: Let \(f(x,y)=x^2y+e^{xy}\). Find \(\nabla f(1,0)\). Then find the directional derivative at \((1,0)\) in the direction \(u=\left(\frac35,\frac45\right)\).
problems.whatToSayBefore: Say: the gradient is made from the two partial derivatives. Directional derivative is gradient dot unit direction.

drawInstructions: Draw the gradient straight upward because \nabla f(1,0)=(0,2).
Draw the unit direction u angled up and right.
Explain the dot product measures how much u points along the gradient.

commonMistakes: Forgetting the derivative of \(e^{xy}\) needs chain rule.
Taking the magnitude of the gradient instead of dotting with \(u\).

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 7 for gradients and directional derivatives.

problems.title: Tangent plane and linear approximation
problems.studentPromptLatex: Let \(f(x,y)=x^2+xy+2y^2\). Find the tangent plane to \(z=f(x,y)\) at \((1,1)\). Then use it to approximate \(f(1.1,0.9)\).
problems.whatToSayBefore: Say: tangent plane is just the linear approximation near the point. Compute height and slopes.

drawInstructions: Draw a flat tangent plane touching the surface at that point.
Show the nearby point (1.1,0.9) using the plane, not the full surface.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to compute \(f(1,1)\), the height of the tangent plane.
Thinking the approximation must equal the exact value.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 7 for tangent planes and linearization.

problems.title: Critical point classification
problems.studentPromptLatex: Find and classify the critical point of \(f(x,y)=x^2+xy+y^2-3x-3y\).
problems.whatToSayBefore: Say: a critical point happens where both first partials are zero. Then the Hessian tells us the shape.

drawInstructions: Draw bowl-shaped level curves centered near (1,1).
Label the Hessian test: D=3>0 and f_xx=2>0, so local min.

walkthrough.label: Set both equal to zero
walkthrough.latex: \[2x+y-3=0,\qquad x+2y-3=0.\] Solving gives \(x=1\), \(y=1\). So the critical point is \((1,1)\).

walkthrough.label: Use the second derivative test
walkthrough.latex: \[D=f_{xx}f_{yy}-(f_{xy})^2=2\cdot2-1^2=3.\] Since \(D>0\) and \(f_{xx}>0\), the critical point is a local minimum.

walkthrough.label: Find the minimum value if needed

problems.finalAnswerLatex: \[\text{Critical point: }(1,1).\quad \text{It is a local minimum, with value }-3.\]
commonMistakes: Mixing up the cases in the second derivative test.
Forgetting that \(D>0\) needs \(f_{xx}\) to decide min vs. max.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 7 for optimization and critical points.

problems.qualityCorrection: Corrected minimum value from -2 to -3 after review.

wrapUpQuestions: Why does the directional derivative use a dot product?
What are the three ingredients of a tangent plane?
What does the second derivative test tell us geometrically?
qualityControlNotes: Checked gradient for f=x^2y+e^(xy): grad(1,0)=(0,2), D_u=8/5.
Checked tangent plane z=4+3(x-1)+5(y-1) and approximation 3.8.
Checked critical point (1,1), Hessian determinant 3, local minimum, value -3.
All direction vectors in directional derivative language are unit vectors.
reviewSummary: Session 17 is ready with simple teaching language and exact visual specs for gradient, tangent plane, and optimization sketches. Minimum value correction applied after audit.
